questions.explanation: The Government of India Act of 1858 transferred the rule of India from the East India Company to the British Crown.

questions.question: Who was the last Governor of the English East India Company appointed by the British Crown before the Company's rule ended?
questions.explanation: Lord Canning was the last Governor-General of the East India Company and the first Viceroy of India after the 1858 Government of India Act.

questions.question: The 'Wood's Despatch' of 1854 is often called the 'Magna Carta of Indian Education'.
questions.explanation: Wood's Despatch recommended a system of education from primary to university level, and stressed on practical and scientific education.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the development of industries: (i) The first jute mill was set up in Bengal in 1855. (ii) The first railway line was laid between Bombay and Thane in 1853. (iii) The first cotton mill was in Calcutta.
questions.explanation: The first jute mill opened in Bengal in 1855, the first railway in 1853 between Bombay and Thane, but the first cotton mill was in Bombay, not Calcutta.

questions.question: The Battle of Plassey (1757) was fought between the British East India Company and which Nawab of Bengal?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Plassey was fought between the British East India Company and Siraj-ud-Daulah, the Nawab of Bengal, in 1757.

questions.question: The Wood's Despatch (1854) is considered the 'Magna Carta' of which field in India?
questions.explanation: Wood's Despatch of 1854 laid the foundation for modern education in India, recommending a system of education from primary to university level.

questions.question: Under the Permanent Settlement, who were recognized as the owners of land?
questions.explanation: The Permanent Settlement (1793) made Zamindars the hereditary owners of land, responsible for collecting revenue.

questions.question: Which official document introduced English education in India in 1835?
questions.explanation: Macaulay's Minute of 1835 advocated for English as the medium of instruction in India.

questions.question: Which city was developed as the capital of British India in 1911?
questions.explanation: In 1911, the British shifted the capital from Calcutta to Delhi, and Delhi was developed as the new capital.

questions.question: Who was the first woman to become the president of the Indian National Congress?
questions.explanation: Annie Besant became the first woman president of the Indian National Congress in 1917.

questions.question: Which Governor General introduced the policy of 'Subsidiary Alliance' in India?
questions.explanation: Lord Wellesley introduced the Subsidiary Alliance system to bring Indian states under British control.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the introduction of Railways in India is correct?
options: The first railway line in India was laid between Bombay and Thane in 1853.
The first railway line in India was laid between Calcutta and Delhi in 1855.
Railways were introduced by Lord Cornwallis.
The first railway line connected Madras and Bangalore.
questions.explanation: The first railway line in India was opened in 1853 between Bombay (Bori Bunder) and Thane.

questions.question: The Indigo Revolt (1859-60) took place in which part of India?
questions.explanation: The Indigo Revolt of 1859-60 took place in Bengal against the oppressive indigo plantation system.

questions.question: Which of the following social evils was abolished by Lord William Bentinck in 1829?
questions.explanation: Lord William Bentinck passed the Regulation XVII in 1829 making the practice of Sati illegal and punishable.

questions.question: The first international telegraph line from India was laid to connect with which country?
questions.explanation: The first international telegraph line from India was laid in 1870 connecting India with England via the Red Sea.

questions.question: The Queen's Proclamation of 1858 announced that India would be governed directly in the name of the British Crown.
questions.explanation: After the 1857 Revolt, the Government of India Act 1858 transferred the control from the East India Company to the British Crown.

questions.question: The Charter Act of 1813 granted permission to Christian missionaries to propagate their religion in India.
questions.explanation: The Charter Act of 1813 allowed Christian missionaries to enter British India for religious work.

questions.question: Which of the following industries was the first to be established in modern India?
questions.explanation: The first modern cotton textile mill was established in Bombay in 1854.

questions.question: In which year was the first modern census conducted in India?
questions.explanation: The first systematic modern census in India was conducted in 1871.

questions.question: Identify the correct pair: 1. Madras School Book Society - 1827 2. Wood's Despatch - 1854 3. Hunter Commission - 1902 4. Sargent Plan - 1929
questions.explanation: Madras School Book Society was established in 1827; Wood's Despatch was in 1854. The Hunter Commission was in 1882, and Sargent Plan was in 1944.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a reason for the development of industries in India during the British period? A) Availability of raw materials B) Cheap labor C) Government support for Indian entrepreneurs D) Development of railways
questions.explanation: The British government did not support Indian entrepreneurs; instead, they promoted British industries and discouraged Indian competition.

questions.question: Which European power established the first trading post in India in 1498?
questions.explanation: The Portuguese under Vasco da Gama reached Calicut in 1498, establishing the first European trading post in India.

questions.question: Which Mughal emperor granted the Diwani of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa to the British East India Company in 1765?
questions.explanation: After the Battle of Buxar, the Treaty of Allahabad (1765) gave the Diwani rights to the British under Shah Alam II.

questions.question: Which university was established first in India in 1857 according to the recommendations of Wood's Despatch?
questions.explanation: The three universities (Calcutta, Bombay, Madras) were founded in 1857 based on the Wood's Despatch.

questions.question: Which social reformer founded the 'Arya Samaj' in 1875?
questions.explanation: Arya Samaj was founded by Dayanand Saraswati in 1875 to reform Hinduism.

questions.question: Who was the Portuguese explorer who discovered the sea route to India in 1498?
questions.explanation: Vasco da Gama reached Calicut in 1498, establishing the first direct sea route from Europe to India.

questions.question: Which city was the capital of British India until 1911?
questions.explanation: Calcutta served as the capital of British India from 1772 until it was shifted to Delhi in 1911.

questions.question: Which European navigator discovered the sea route to India in 1498?
questions.explanation: Vasco da Gama reached Calicut in 1498, discovering the sea route to India.

questions.question: The document that laid the foundation for modern education in India was:
questions.explanation: Wood's Despatch (1854) recommended the establishment of universities and a systematic education system.

questions.question: Which Act passed by the British in 1858 transferred the power of the East India Company to the British Crown?
questions.explanation: The Government of India Act of 1858 abolished the East India Company and transferred the control of India to the British Crown.

questions.question: Who authored the book 'The History of British India'?
questions.explanation: James Mill, a British historian, wrote 'The History of British India' in 1817, which was a standard textbook for Indian Civil Service.

questions.question: The first Indian city to have a municipality was Madras (Chennai). (True/False)
questions.explanation: Madras established the first municipal corporation in India in 1688, as covered in Unit 7 'Urban changes during the British period'.

questions.question: The helpline number for children below 18 years who feel unsafe is 1098.
questions.explanation: As per the textbook front matter, 1098 is the toll-free helpline for children in distress.

questions.question: Which social reformer actively campaigned for the abolition of the practice of Sati?
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy persistently opposed Sati and his efforts led to the Bengal Sati Regulation of 1829, which declared the practice illegal.

questions.question: Which of the following was the main reason for the advent of the Europeans to India?
options: To trade in spices and other goods
questions.explanation: The primary motive for the arrival of European trading companies like the Portuguese, Dutch, English, and French was to trade in valuable Indian spices, textiles, and other goods.

questions.question: Where was the first English factory in India established?
questions.explanation: The first English factory was established at Surat in 1612 by the East India Company.

questions.question: The introduction of English as a medium of instruction in India was recommended by which Governor-General’s advisor in his famous 'Minute'?
questions.explanation: Thomas Babington Macaulay's Minute of 1835, under Governor-General Lord William Bentinck, advocated English education in India. Lord Dalhousie introduced other reforms, while Wellesley and Cornwallis were earlier Governor-Generals.

questions.question: The Widow Remarriage Act of 1856 was passed largely due to the persistent efforts of which social reformer?
questions.explanation: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar campaigned vigorously for widow remarriage, leading to the Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act of 1856. Raja Rammohan Roy worked against sati, while Dayananda Saraswati and Keshab Chandra Sen were also reformers but not primarily for this Act.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major reason for the British to introduce Western education in India?
options: To promote Indian languages and culture
To create a class of Indians who would assist in administration
To spread Christianity among the masses
To reduce the tax burden on Indian peasants
questions.explanation: The British introduced Western education to create a class of Indians who would be Indian in blood and colour but English in taste, opinions, morals, and intellect, to assist in administration.

questions.question: Which battle gave the English East India Company the right to collect revenue (diwani) in Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa?
questions.explanation: After the Battle of Buxar (1764), the Mughal emperor Shah Alam II granted the Diwani rights of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa to the Company in 1765.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a result of British land revenue policies in India?
questions.explanation: British revenue policies led to the rise of zamindars, commercialization of agriculture, and deindustrialization, but did not promote industrialization in India.

questions.question: The system of 'Ryotwari' settlement was primarily introduced by whom in British India?
questions.explanation: The Ryotwari system, where revenue was directly collected from the peasants (ryots), was introduced by Thomas Munro in the Madras Presidency.

questions.question: Assertion (A): The British introduced railways in India primarily for administrative and military purposes. Reason (R): Railways helped in the quick movement of troops and raw materials.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The British introduced railways to consolidate their control and facilitate the movement of troops and goods, so both statements are true and R correctly explains A.

questions.question: Statement: The British introduced railways in India primarily for the benefit of Indian trade and commerce. True or False?
questions.explanation: Railways were introduced mainly to facilitate British economic exploitation and military control, not for Indian benefit.

questions.question: True or False: The 'Indian Universities Act' of 1904 was passed to improve the standard of university education and bring them under stricter government control.
questions.explanation: The Indian Universities Act of 1904 aimed to increase government control over universities and improve the quality of education.

questions.question: The 'Sati Abolition Act' was passed in 1829 due to the efforts of which social reformer?
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy campaigned against the practice of Sati, leading to its abolition by Lord William Bentinck in 1829.

questions.question: The Battle of Plassey (1757) is a pivotal event in the establishment of British rule in India. Which of the following statements about this battle is correct?
options: It was fought between the English East India Company and the French East India Company.
Robert Clive conspired with Mir Jafar, the commander of the Nawab of Bengal's army, to defeat Siraj-ud-Daulah.
The immediate cause of the battle was the English refusal to stop minting coins at the Calcutta mint.
The battle resulted in the English gaining the Diwani rights of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa.
questions.explanation: The Battle of Plassey was fought between the English East India Company and Siraj-ud-Daulah, the Nawab of Bengal. Robert Clive secured the support of Mir Jafar, the Nawab's commander, by promising him the throne. This betrayal led to the English victory.

questions.question: The introduction of the English education system in India was a deliberate colonial strategy. Which of the following statements best reflects the objective of Lord Macaulay's 'Minute on Indian Education' (1835)?
options: To promote a blend of Oriental and Western learning.
To create a class of Indians who would be Indian in blood and colour, but English in taste, opinions, morals, and intellect.
To revive the traditional Indian systems of education like the Madrasa and Pathshala.
To provide free and compulsory primary education to all Indian children.
questions.explanation: Macaulay's Minute explicitly aimed at creating a class of 'interpreters' who would be Indian by birth but English in their thinking, to serve as a bridge between the British rulers and the masses they governed.

questions.question: The development of modern industries in India during the British period led to the decline of traditional handicrafts. Which of the following was NOT a reason for this decline?
options: Disappearance of princely courts and rich patrons.
Heavy duties imposed on Indian goods in European markets.
Introduction of machine-made goods from England.
The British policy of providing active state support to Indian artisans.
questions.explanation: The British did not support Indian artisans. Instead, their policies, such as free trade and heavy duties on Indian goods in Britain, actively destroyed the traditional handicraft industry.

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Lapse' was a policy used by Lord Dalhousie to annex Indian states. Which of the following states was annexed under this doctrine?
questions.explanation: The Doctrine of Lapse stated that if a ruler of a princely state died without a natural heir, the state would 'lapse' to the British. Satara was the first state to be annexed under this doctrine in 1848.

questions.question: The British introduced several changes in the urban landscape of India. Which of the following was a characteristic feature of colonial urban development?
options: Development of traditional 'mandi' towns around marketplaces.
Creation of 'dual cities' with a European cantonment and a native 'Black Town'.
Focus on building universities and public libraries in the city centers.
Promotion of small-scale, decentralized industrial units within the city.
questions.explanation: Colonial cities like Madras, Bombay, and Calcutta were characterized by a dual structure: a well-planned, spacious area for Europeans (cantonment/civil lines) and a crowded, unplanned area for the native population (Black Town).

questions.question: The social reform movements in 19th century India aimed at improving the status of women. Which of the following reformers was associated with the founding of the 'Arya Samaj'?
questions.explanation: Swami Dayanand Saraswati founded the Arya Samaj in 1875. The Samaj worked for social reform, including the promotion of women's education and the abolition of caste discrimination.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding the urban changes under British rule? i) The three Presidency towns were Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras. ii) The British introduced railways and municipalities. iii) Hill stations like Simla were developed as summer capitals. iv) All of the above
questions.explanation: All statements are correct: Presidency towns (Calcutta, Bombay, Madras), railways and municipalities, hill stations like Simla.

questions.question: The Battle of Buxar (1764) was fought between the combined forces of the Mughal Emperor and Nawab of Bengal against the British East India Company. (True/False)
questions.explanation: The combined forces of Mir Qasim (Nawab of Bengal), Shuja-ud-Daulah (Nawab of Awadh), and Shah Alam II (Mughal Emperor) fought against the British at Buxar.

questions.question: The Treaty of Srirangapatnam concluded which of the following wars between the British East India Company and Mysore?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Srirangapatnam was signed in 1792, concluding the Third Anglo-Mysore War between the British East India Company and Tipu Sultan.

questions.question: Statement I: The Wood's Dispatch of 1854 is considered the 'Magna Carta of English Education in India'. Statement II: It recommended the creation of a separate department of public instruction and the establishment of universities in Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras.
options: Statement I is true, Statement II is false
Statement I is false, Statement II is true
Both statements are true and Statement II correctly explains Statement I
Both statements are true but Statement II is not the correct explanation of Statement I
questions.explanation: The Wood's Dispatch is indeed hailed as the Magna Carta of English education. It explicitly recommended a Department of Public Instruction and the founding of universities in the three Presidency towns, setting up a coordinated education system. Thus, both statements are true and II explains I.

questions.question: Identify the Social Reform Movement that campaigned against the caste-based discrimination of 'Avarnas' and led to the formation of the 'Satyashodhak Samaj'.
questions.explanation: The Self-Respect Movement, led by Periyar E. V. Ramasamy, actively fought against caste discrimination. While the Satyashodhak Samaj was founded earlier by Jyotiba Phule, Periyar's movement was deeply connected to the rationalist and anti-caste struggle in Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: Which of the following Acts made the Governor-General of Bengal the 'Governor-General of India' and vested all civil and military powers in him?
questions.explanation: The Charter Act of 1833 transformed the Governor-General of Bengal into the Governor-General of India (with Lord William Bentinck being the first) and centralized authority, ending the trading activities of the East India Company and making it purely an administrative body.

questions.question: What was the primary objective of the 'Tamil Nadu Archives' and the 'Connemara Library', established during the British period in Madras?
options: Preserving official records and providing public access to books
questions.explanation: The Connemara Library (established 1896) was a public library aimed at providing access to books, while the Tamil Nadu Archives preserved important official records of the administration. Both were part of British efforts in record-keeping and education in the Madras Presidency.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'Drain of Wealth' theory associated with British rule in India?
options: The transfer of capital from India to Britain without adequate returns
The construction of irrigation canals draining river water
The migration of Indian labourers to British colonies
The excessive taxation on Indian farmers
questions.explanation: The 'Drain of Wealth' theory, articulated by Dadabhai Naoroji, argued that a significant portion of India's national wealth was being transferred to Britain as home charges, salaries, profits, and other expenses, with no corresponding economic benefit to India.

questions.question: True or False: The 'Vernacular Press Act' of 1878 was designed to protect Indian language newspapers from government censorship.
questions.explanation: False. The Vernacular Press Act of 1878 was specifically aimed at 'better control' of publications in Indian languages. It gave the government extensive powers to confiscate newspapers and impose fines, effectively suppressing criticism in the vernacular press.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Wood's Dispatch of 1854: (i) It recommended the establishment of universities in India. (ii) It proposed the creation of a Department of Education. (iii) It emphasised female education. (iv) It recommended English as the medium of instruction at all levels. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Wood's Dispatch recommended universities (Calcutta, Bombay, Madras), a Department of Education, and female education, but not English at all levels—it supported vernacular medium at lower levels.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a Presidency town under British India?
questions.explanation: The three Presidency towns were Madras, Bombay, and Calcutta. Karachi was part of the Bombay Presidency but not a Presidency town itself.

questions.question: Who founded the 'Arya Mahila Samaj' in 1882 to promote women's education and social reform?
questions.explanation: Pandita Ramabai founded the Arya Mahila Samaj in Pune in 1882 to work for women's education and against child marriage.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the urbanisation under British rule is correct?
options: Hill stations like Simla were developed primarily for trade and commerce.
Presidency towns were originally fortified trading posts of the East India Company.
The British introduced planned cities like Delhi after 1857.
Municipal corporations were first created in the 20th century.
questions.explanation: Presidency towns (Madras, Bombay, Calcutta) grew from fortified factories of the East India Company. Hill stations were for recreation, not trade. Delhi was not planned by British initially. Municipal corporations existed from the 18th century.

questions.question: Which of the following European powers established the first factory in India at Surat in 1612?
questions.explanation: The English East India Company established its first factory at Surat in 1612 after obtaining permission from the Mughal emperor Jahangir.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the development of industries under British rule is correct? (i) The British encouraged modern industries to compete with British goods. (ii) The British imposed heavy tariffs on Indian goods. (iii) The first jute mill was established in Bengal. (iv) The Industrial Revolution in Britain had no impact on Indian industries.
questions.explanation: Statement (i) is false – British discouraged Indian industries; (ii) true – heavy tariffs on Indian goods; (iii) true – first jute mill in Bengal in 1855; (iv) false – Industrial Revolution destroyed Indian handicrafts.

questions.question: Which of the following is known as the Magna Carta of English Education in India?
questions.explanation: Wood's Despatch of 1854 recommended a comprehensive system of education from primary to university level and is often called the Magna Carta of English Education in India.

questions.question: Who among the following was the founder of the French East India Company?
questions.explanation: The French East India Company was founded by Jean-Baptiste Colbert in 1664.

questions.question: Which of the following was a consequence of deindustrialisation under British rule?
options: Growth of modern industries in India
questions.explanation: British policies led to the decline of traditional handicrafts and the destruction of India's rural industries, a process known as deindustrialisation.

questions.question: Which of the following were the objectives of Wood's Despatch (1854)? (i) Promotion of Western education (ii) Establishment of universities (iii) Grant-in-aid to private schools (iv) Restricting education to upper castes
questions.explanation: Wood's Despatch recommended western education, university establishment, and grant-in-aid system; it did not restrict to upper castes.

questions.question: Who among the following was the Governor-General of India when the Doctrine of Lapse was vigorously applied?
questions.explanation: Lord Dalhousie (1848-1856) implemented the Doctrine of Lapse, annexing states like Satara, Jhansi, and Nagpur.

questions.question: Match the social reformers with their contributions: A) Raja Rammohan Roy - 1) Founded the Arya Samaj, B) Dayananda Saraswati - 2) Abolition of Sati, C) Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar - 3) Widow Remarriage Act, D) Pandita Ramabai - 4) Founded the Sharda Sadan.
questions.explanation: Raja Rammohan Roy campaigned against Sati; Dayananda founded Arya Samaj; Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar pushed for Widow Remarriage Act 1856; Pandita Ramabai established Sharda Sadan for widows.

questions.question: During British rule, the construction of the Grand Trunk Road from Calcutta to Peshawar was completed under the administration of which Governor-General?
questions.explanation: Lord Dalhousie oversaw the completion of the Grand Trunk Road, improving connectivity and trade.

questions.question: Which British educational despatch is often called the 'Magna Carta of Indian Education'?
questions.explanation: Wood's Despatch of 1854 is regarded as the 'Magna Carta of Indian Education' as it laid the foundation for a coordinated education system.

questions.question: Which hill station was developed as the summer capital of British India?
questions.explanation: Shimla served as the summer capital of British India from 1864 onwards.

questions.question: The first jute mill in India was established in 1855 near ____________.
questions.explanation: The first jute mill was set up at Rishra near Calcutta (Kolkata) in 1855.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Battle of Plassey (1757) are correct? (i) It was fought between the British East India Company and Siraj-ud-Daulah. (ii) Robert Clive led the British forces. (iii) Mir Jafar betrayed Siraj-ud-Daulah. (iv) The battle resulted in the British gaining control of Bengal.
questions.explanation: All statements are correct. The Battle of Plassey was a turning point in British colonial rule.

questions.question: The first cotton textile mill in India was established in which year and location?
questions.explanation: The first modern cotton textile mill was set up in Bombay in 1854 by Cowasji Nanabhai Davar.

questions.question: Which of the following hill stations was developed by the British as a summer retreat for the government?
questions.explanation: Shimla became the official summer capital of the British Indian government from 1864.

questions.question: Which of the following acts introduced the system of dual government in Bengal?
options: The system was introduced by Robert Clive in 1765
questions.explanation: Robert Clive introduced the dual system of government in Bengal after the Treaty of Allahabad (1765), where the Company had Diwani rights but the Nizamat was with the Nawab.

questions.question: The Dual System of Government in Bengal was introduced by which Governor?
questions.explanation: Robert Clive introduced the Dual System of Government in Bengal after the Battle of Plassey (1757), where the Company controlled revenue (diwani) and the Nawab handled administration (nizamat).

questions.question: Which of the following were causes for the Revolt of 1857? (i) Doctrine of Lapse, (ii) General Service Enlistment Act, (iii) Introduction of railways and telegraph, (iv) Social reforms of British.
questions.explanation: All four factors contributed: Doctrine of Lapse (political), General Service Enlistment Act (grievances of sepoys), railways/telegraph (perceived disruption), and social reforms (e.g., abolition of Sati) were causes of the revolt.

questions.question: In the context of the British revenue systems in India, which system was characterized by the settlement of revenue directly with the individual cultivator, bypassing intermediaries, and was primarily implemented in the Madras Presidency?
questions.explanation: The Ryotwari System, introduced by Thomas Munro, involved direct settlement between the government and the ryot (cultivator). It was extensively applied in the Madras Presidency.

questions.question: In the context of the development of industries in India under British rule, which of the following statements about the growth of the cotton textile industry is correct?
options: The first cotton mill in India was established in Bombay in 1854.
The cotton textile industry remained confined to the port cities due to the availability of raw cotton and export facilities.
The British government actively promoted the growth of Indian cotton mills to compete with Lancashire.
Both A and B are correct.
questions.explanation: The first cotton mill was set up in Bombay (1854), and the industry was concentrated in port cities like Bombay, Ahmedabad, and Calcutta for logistical reasons. The British did not actively promote Indian mills; in fact, they often imposed discriminatory tariffs.

questions.question: Under the Dual Government system introduced by Robert Clive in Bengal, the administrative responsibilities were divided between the Company and the Nawab. Which of the following correctly describes the division of powers?
options: The Company held the Nizamat (military and police) while the Nawab held the Diwani (revenue).
The Company held the Diwani (revenue) while the Nawab held the Nizamat (military and police).
Both powers were held by the Nawab, with the Company acting as a subsidiary ally.
The Company held both Diwani and Nizamat, leaving the Nawab with only judicial powers.
questions.explanation: In the Dual Government (1765), the Company acquired the Diwani (right to collect revenue) from the Mughal emperor, while the Nawab continued to exercise Nizamat (military and criminal justice), though in practice the Company controlled both.

questions.question: The Doctrine of Lapse was used by the British to annex several princely states. Which of the following states was NOT annexed under the Doctrine of Lapse?
questions.explanation: Punjab was annexed in 1849 after the Second Anglo-Sikh War, not through the Doctrine of Lapse. Satara (1848), Nagpur (1853), and Jhansi (1854) were annexed under the Doctrine.

questions.question: The 'Subsidiary Alliance' system was a major instrument of British expansion. Under this system, the Indian ruler was required to maintain a British force in his territory and pay for it. Which of the following was a consequence of the Subsidiary Alliance for the Indian state?
options: The Indian ruler gained full autonomy in external affairs.
The Indian ruler could no longer maintain diplomatic relations with other states without British approval.
The Indian ruler was allowed to raise a large army independent of British control.
The Indian ruler was exempted from paying subsidies to the British.
questions.explanation: Under the Subsidiary Alliance, the Indian ruler had to disband his own army, accept a British Resident, and could not enter into alliances or wage war without British permission, effectively losing sovereignty.

questions.question: In the context of urban changes during the British period, the city of Madras (Chennai) developed around the Fort St. George. Which of the following best describes the pattern of urban growth in Madras under British rule?
options: The city grew organically with no planned expansion, leading to congested slums near the port.
The British built a planned 'White Town' near the fort for Europeans and a 'Black Town' for Indians, with racial segregation.
Madras was developed as a hill station for British officials seeking cooler climate.
The city expanded primarily through the establishment of textile mills in the outskirts.
questions.explanation: Madras, like other presidency cities, had a clear racial divide: the 'White Town' near Fort St. George housed Europeans, while the 'Black Town' (later George Town) was for Indians, reflecting colonial urban planning.

questions.question: The Indian Councils Act of 1861 allowed Indians to participate in the legislative process for the first time. True or False?
questions.explanation: True. The Indian Councils Act of 1861 included nominated Indian members to the Viceroy's Legislative Council, but their role was advisory only.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a cause of the Revolt of 1857? (A) Greased cartridges incident, (B) Doctrine of Lapse, (C) Introduction of railways, (D) Social reforms like Abolition of Sati.
options: Social reforms like Abolition of Sati
questions.explanation: The introduction of railways was not a direct cause of the revolt; it was actually a means of communication used by the British to suppress revolts. The other three were key triggers.

questions.question: Who wrote the 'Minutes on Education' in 1835 that advocated introducing English education in India?
questions.explanation: Lord Macaulay's Minute on Education (1835) strongly favoured English as the medium of instruction to create a class of Indians who would serve the British.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the status of women in 19th century India: (i) Sati was abolished by Lord William Bentinck in 1829. (ii) The Widow Remarriage Act was passed in 1856. (iii) The Age of Consent Act raised the marriage age of girls to 18. Which are correct?
questions.explanation: Sati abolition (1829) and Widow Remarriage Act (1856) are correct, but the Age of Consent Act (1891) raised the age to 12, not 18.

questions.question: The First Anglo-Maratha War ended with the Treaty of ________.
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Salbai (1782) ended the First Anglo-Maratha War, restoring the status quo and allowing the British to consolidate power.

questions.question: Which of the following acts raised the age of consent for marriage for girls from 10 to 12 years in 1891?
questions.explanation: The Age of Consent Act, 1891, raised the age of consent for girls from 10 to 12 years. It was a significant reform regarding the status of women.

questions.question: The 'Charter Act' of 1813 is significant in the history of Indian education because it:
options: Completely stopped all missionary activities in India.
Allocated a sum of one lakh rupees for the revival and promotion of literature and learning.
Established the first university in India.
Introduced the Macaulay's Minute on Education.
questions.explanation: The Charter Act of 1813 directed the Company to spend one lakh rupees for the promotion of education in India, marking a shift towards state responsibility for education.

questions.question: The 'Indigo Revolt' (1859-60) was primarily a peasant movement against the oppressive practices of the indigo planters. In which region of India did this revolt mainly occur?
questions.explanation: The Indigo Revolt took place in Bengal (specifically in the Nadia district). Peasants refused to grow indigo under the exploitative system forced by European planters.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the status of women in 19th century India: (i) The practice of sati was declared illegal in 1829 through the efforts of Lord William Bentinck and Raja Rammohan Roy. (ii) The Age of Consent Act (1891) raised the age of marriage for girls to 12 years. (iii) The Bhakti movement had no impact on improving women's status. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Sati was abolished in 1829; Age of Consent Act 1891 raised marriage age to 12; statement (iii) is false – the Bhakti movement promoted gender equality.

questions.question: The introduction of railways in India (covered in 'Urban changes during the British period') was primarily intended to promote Indian industrial development. (True/False)
questions.explanation: Railways were built to serve British economic interests—transporting raw materials and troops—not to promote Indian industry.

questions.question: In the chapter 'Rural Life and Society', the Permanent Settlement system made the _________ the tax collectors of Bengal.
questions.explanation: Under the Permanent Settlement, Zamindars were made hereditary revenue collectors responsible for paying fixed revenue to the British.

questions.question: Consider the following statements from the chapter 'Status of Women in India through the ages': (i) Sati was abolished in 1829. (ii) Widow remarriage was legalized by the Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act of 1856. (iii) The Age of Consent Act raised the marriage age for girls to 14. (iv) The first women's university was established in Calcutta. Which is/are correct?
questions.explanation: (i), (ii), (iii) are correct. The first women's university was the SNDT Women's University in Bombay, not Calcutta.

questions.question: Assertion (A): The Ryotwari System introduced by the British in Madras Presidency fixed a high revenue demand on cultivators. Reason (R): Under Ryotwari, the settlement was made directly with the ryot (cultivator) and was revised periodically. Choose the correct option.
options: A is true but R is false
Both A & R are true and R explains A
Both A & R are true but R does not explain A
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Under Ryotwari, land revenue was settled directly with the cultivator and was periodically revised, leading to high demands. R correctly explains why the system became oppressive.

questions.question: Which city overhauled its urban planning under the British by creating the ‘Black Town’ and ‘White Town’ divisions, leading to racial segregation of residential areas?
questions.explanation: In Madras, the British established ‘Black Town’ for Indians and ‘White Town’ for Europeans, creating distinct racial urban zones.

questions.question: In the context of the 1857 Revolt, what role did the ‘Dhak’ (drumbeat) play in spreading the rebellion in the villages of northern India?
options: It was used to announce tax collection dates
It served as a signal to gather villagers for revolt against British officials
It was a ritual beat to welcome British officers
It was a form of entertainment in village fairs
questions.explanation: The Dhak beat was used as a traditional means of mass communication to gather villagers, especially to mobilise them against the British during the 1857 revolt.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the status of women in early medieval India is correct?
options: Women enjoyed complete equality in property rights
The practice of sati became widespread among certain upper castes
Girls were compulsorily educated in temple schools
questions.explanation: In early medieval India, the practice of sati, especially among Rajput and upper caste women, became more prevalent as a symbol of honour, while women's rights generally declined.

questions.question: Which British missionary institution in Calcutta played a pioneering role in the education of girls in early 19th century India by establishing schools for Indian girls?
options: The Church Missionary Society’s girls school
questions.explanation: The Serampore Mission, established by William Carey and other Baptist missionaries, set up several schools for girls in and around Calcutta, including early girls' education initiatives.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about urbanization under the British: I. Bombay grew as a major cotton textile centre. II. Calcutta was the capital of British India until 1911. III. Madras developed primarily as a port for the export of cotton and hides. Which of these statements are correct?
questions.explanation: All three statements are correct. Bombay became the cotton textile hub; Calcutta served as the British capital till 1911; Madras was a major export port for raw cotton, hides, and skins.

questions.question: Which Governor-General introduced the ‘economic drain theory’ in official correspondence to highlight the outflow of wealth from India to Britain during the late 18th century?
questions.explanation: Warren Hastings, in his official dispatches, described the massive transfer of Indian wealth to Britain as a ‘drain’, long before Dadabhai Naoroji formalized the theory.

questions.question: True or False: The Hunter Commission (1882-83) recommended that primary education be provided only in English to promote Western learning in India.
questions.explanation: The Hunter Commission actually recommended that primary education be imparted through the vernacular language to make it more effective and accessible to the masses, not exclusively in English.

questions.question: Which of the following European powers was the first to establish a factory in India, specifically in Calicut in 1500?
questions.explanation: The Portuguese, led by Vasco da Gama, arrived in Calicut in 1498 and established a factory there by 1500.

questions.question: The Battle of Plassey (1757) was fought between the English East India Company and which Nawab of Bengal?
questions.explanation: Siraj-ud-daulah was the Nawab of Bengal at the time of the Battle of Plassey, which marked the beginning of British political dominance in India.

questions.question: The famous quote 'The bird that does not eat, does not fly, will go to heaven' was used by whom to justify a particular British policy in India?
questions.explanation: Lord Dalhousie used this quote to justify the Doctrine of Lapse, arguing that states without a natural heir would be annexed.

questions.question: The 'Tanjore Peasant Revolt' of the late 18th century was primarily against which oppressive practice?
options: High land revenue demands and mirasidari system
questions.explanation: The Tanjore revolt was against the oppressive mirasidari (landlord) system and high revenue demands by the British.

questions.question: The first women's university in India, SNDT, was established in:
questions.explanation: Shreemati Nathibai Damodar Thackersey (SNDT) Women's University was founded in 1916 by Dhondo Keshav Karve.

questions.question: Under the Permanent Settlement of 1793, what percentage of the rent collected was fixed as the revenue payable by the zamindars to the British government?
questions.explanation: The Permanent Settlement fixed the zamindar's revenue at 89% of the rental income, leaving only 11% for the zamindar.

questions.question: Who among the following was the first Indian to graduate from the University of Calcutta?
questions.explanation: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar was the first Indian to graduate from the University of Calcutta (in 1841, though the university was founded later; he had already passed the senior scholarship exam).

questions.question: Who established the first iron and steel works in India at Kulti in 1870?
questions.explanation: James Erskine (of the Bengal Iron Works) established the first iron and steel factory at Kulti in 1870.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major factor for the growth of nationalism in India during the British period?
questions.explanation: Railways, Western education, and economic exploitation all contributed to the rise of nationalism by unifying people and exposing colonial injustices.

questions.question: Statement: The 'Magna Carta of Indian Education' refers to which document?
questions.explanation: Wood's Despatch of 1854 is called the Magna Carta of Indian Education as it laid the foundation for a coordinated education system.

questions.question: True or False: The 'Sati' practice was legally abolished in India in 1829 due to the efforts of Raja Rammohan Roy.
questions.explanation: Lord William Bentinck abolished Sati in 1829, with strong support from social reformers like Raja Rammohan Roy.

questions.question: Which of the following urban changes during the British period led to the development of hill stations like Ooty and Shimla?
options: Need for administrative and recreational centers
questions.explanation: Hill stations were developed by the British as summer retreats and administrative centers to escape the heat of the plains.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a reason for the advent of Europeans to India?
options: To learn Indian languages and culture
questions.explanation: The primary motives of Europeans were trade, colonization, and missionary activities, not learning Indian languages and culture.

questions.question: Who among the following was associated with the development of education in India and founded the Hindu College in Calcutta?
questions.explanation: David Hare, a Scottish watchmaker, co-founded the Hindu College (later Presidency College) in Calcutta in 1817.

questions.question: Which of the following urban changes occurred during the British period in India?
options: Development of hill stations and cantonments
questions.explanation: The British developed hill stations (e.g., Ooty, Simla) and cantonments for administrative and recreational purposes.

questions.question: True or False: The 'Indian Councils Act of 1892' introduced the principle of direct election for the first time in India.
questions.explanation: The Act of 1892 introduced indirect election, not direct election. Direct election came later with the Act of 1909 (Morley-Minto Reforms).

questions.question: Which event is considered the beginning of British political power in India?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Plassey in 1757 gave the British East India Company control over Bengal, marking the start of their political dominance in India.

questions.question: Under the Mahalwari system, revenue was collected from whom?
questions.explanation: The Mahalwari system treated the entire village as a unit (mahal) for revenue assessment and collection.

questions.question: Which of the following were features of the British educational policy in India? (i) Promotion of English as medium of instruction. (ii) Establishment of universities in Calcutta, Bombay, Madras. (iii) Encouragement of traditional Sanskrit and Persian education. (iv) Use of education to create a class of Indians who would serve British interests.
questions.explanation: The British promoted English, established universities in 1857, and aimed to produce a class of Indians loyal to British rule; they did not encourage traditional education.

questions.question: The first railway line in India was laid between which stations?
questions.explanation: The first railway line in India opened in 1853 between Bombay (Boribunder) and Thane, covering 34 km.

questions.question: Under the Permanent Settlement of 1793, the zamindars were recognized as proprietors of land. What percentage of the rental was fixed as the Company's share?
questions.explanation: The Permanent Settlement fixed the revenue demand at 10/11 (about 89%) of the rental, leaving 11% to the zamindar.

questions.question: Which book by Dadabhai Naoroji systematically exposed the economic exploitation of India under British rule?
options: Poverty and Un-British Rule in India
questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naoroji's 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India' (1901) argued that British rule drained India's wealth.

questions.question: Which act was passed by the British government in 1850 that allowed conversion to Christianity without losing property rights?
questions.explanation: The Religious Disabilities Act of 1850 was passed by the British to protect the rights of converts to Christianity, allowing them to inherit property despite changing religion.

questions.question: Assertion (A): The British introduced Western education in India. Reason (R): The British wanted to create a class of Indians who would be Indian in blood and colour but English in taste and opinion. Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true
questions.explanation: The British introduced Western education, and Macaulay's minutes clearly stated the aim to create a class that would be Indian in blood but English in taste and opinion.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major development in the field of education during the British period?
questions.explanation: Both Wood's Dispatch of 1854 (which recommended the creation of a system of education) and the Indian Universities Act of 1904 (which reformed university administration) were significant developments.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Vellore Revolt of 1806 was the first major revolt against the British in South India. Statement 2: The revolt was triggered by a ban on religious marks and new dress codes for sepoys. Choose the correct option:
options: Statement 1 is correct, Statement 2 is wrong
Statement 2 is correct, Statement 1 is wrong
questions.explanation: The Vellore Revolt (1806) is considered the first major uprising in South India against the British, and it was sparked by orders banning religious marks and introducing a new turban style for sepoys.

questions.question: ‘Status of Women in India through the ages’ அத்தியாயத்தில், ‘அரண்யகுமாரி (Aranyaka) காலம்’ இல் பெண்கள் பெற்ற முக்கிய சமூக உரிமை எது?
questions.explanation: அரண்யகுமாரி காலத்தில் பெண்கள் சொத்து உரிமை பெற்றனர்.

questions.question: ‘Rural Life and Society’ அத்தியாயத்தின் படி, 19ஆம் நூற்றாண்டில் இந்திய கிராமங்களில் ‘சமுதாயம் (Caste) அமைப்பு’ அதிகமாக பாதித்தது:
questions.explanation: சமுதாயம் கிராம சமூக உறவுகள் மற்றும் திருமண முறைகளை நிர்ணயித்தது.

questions.question: ‘Development of Industries in India’ அத்தியாயத்தில், 1900-களில் ‘சோப்பு (Soap) தொழில்’ முதலில் எங்கு தொடங்கியது?
questions.explanation: மும்பையில் 1900-களில் சோப்பு தொழில் ஆரம்பிக்கப்பட்டது.

questions.question: ‘Rural Life and Society’ அத்தியாயத்தின் படி, 19ஆம் நூற்றாண்டில் இந்திய கிராமங்களில் ‘பஞ்சாயத்து’ அமைப்பின் முதன்மை பணி என்ன?
questions.explanation: பஞ்சாயத்து கிராம நீதித்துறையாக செயல்பட்டது.

questions.question: ‘True/False’ – ‘Status of Women in India through the ages’ அத்தியாயத்தில், ‘பிரிட்டிஷ் காலத்தில் பெண்களுக்கு உயர்நிலைப் பள்ளி கல்வி வழங்கப்பட்டது’ என்பது உண்மை.
questions.explanation: பிரிட்டிஷ் காலத்தில் பெண்களுக்கு உயர்நிலைப் பள்ளி கல்வி அதிகரித்தது, ஆனால் எல்லா பகுதிகளிலும் முழுமையாக இல்லை; இது உண்மை அல்ல.

questions.question: Which European power first established a permanent settlement at Fort St. George in 1639, marking the beginning of the European advent in South India?
questions.explanation: The British East India Company founded Fort St. George in Madras in 1639, initiating British presence in South India.

questions.question: In the chapter ‘Development of Education in India’, which missionary society established the first modern school for girls in India at Bhopal in 1869?
options: Society for the Propagation of the Gospel
questions.explanation: The Methodist Missionary Society started the first modern girls' school in Bhopal.

questions.question: True or False: The ‘Status of Women in India through the ages’ chapter states that women in the Vedic period enjoyed complete equality with men in all spheres of life.
questions.explanation: False. While Vedic women had certain rights, full equality was not universal.

questions.question: Which act introduced the concept of ‘dual government’ in the provinces, separating the executive and legislative functions?
questions.explanation: The Government of India Act 1919 (Montagu‑Chelmsford Reforms) introduced dyarchy.

questions.question: In the context of ‘Rural Life and Society’, which of the following was a direct result of the introduction of the Ryotwari system?
options: Individual land revenue settlement with cultivators
State ownership of all agricultural land
questions.explanation: Ryotwari settled revenue directly with individual cultivators, unlike zamindari.

questions.question: கேள்வி 3: 26 ஜூலை மாத ‘Rural Life and Society’ அத்தியாயத்தில் விவரிக்கப்பட்ட சமூக அமைப்பு எது?
questions.explanation: அத்தியாயம் கிராம சமூகத்தின் குல அமைப்பை விவரிக்கிறது.

questions.question: கேள்வி 5: ‘Development of Education in India’ அத்தியாயத்தில் குறிப்பிடப்பட்ட கல்வி முறை எது?
questions.explanation: அத்தியாயம் அரசு பள்ளிகளை முதன்மையாக விவரிக்கிறது.

questions.question: கேள்வி 6: ‘Development of Industries in India’ அத்தியாயத்தில் குறிப்பிடப்பட்ட முக்கிய தொழில் எது?
questions.explanation: அத்தியாயம் தொழிற்சாலை வளர்ச்சியை குறிப்பிடுகிறது.

questions.question: கேள்வி 8: ‘Status of Women in India through the ages’ அத்தியாயத்தில் குறிப்பிடப்பட்ட முக்கிய நிகழ்வு எது?
questions.explanation: அத்தியாயம் பெண்களின் கல்வி உரிமைகளைப் பற்றியது.

questions.question: கேள்வி 9: ‘Rural Life and Society’ அத்தியாயத்தில் குறிப்பிடப்பட்ட ‘வேளாண்மை’ பற்றிய முக்கிய கருத்து என்ன?
questions.explanation: அத்தியாயம் வேளாண்மையை முக்கியமாக விவரிக்கிறது.

questions.question: கேள்வி 13: ‘Status of Women in India through the ages’ அத்தியாயத்தில் குறிப்பிடப்பட்ட ‘மகளிர் கல்வி’ பற்றிய முக்கிய கருத்து என்ன?
questions.explanation: அத்தியாயம் 19ஆம் நூற்றாண்டில் மகளிர் கல்வி தொடங்கியதை குறிப்பிடுகிறது.

questions.question: ‘Rural Life and Society’ அத்தியாயத்தில் விவரிக்கப்பட்ட ‘பருவப் பணி’ (seasonal labour) பற்றிய கீழ்க்கண்ட கூற்றுகளில் தவறானது எது?
options: இது நில உரிமையாளர்களுக்கு கூடுதல் உழைப்பு பெறும் வழி.
பருவப் பணி செய்யும் தொழிலாளர்கள் நிலம் உரிமை பெறும் வாய்ப்பு அதிகம்.
பருவப் பணி பல குடும்பங்களின் வருமானத்தை நிலைநிறுத்த உதவுகிறது.
questions.explanation: பருவப் பணி செய்யும் தொழிலாளர்கள் நிலம் உரிமை பெறும் வாய்ப்பு குறைவு; அவர்கள் பொதுவாக நில உரிமையற்றவர்கள்.

questions.question: ‘Development of Industries in India’ அத்தியாயத்தின் படி, 1900 களில் இந்தியாவில் முதன்மையாக வளர்ந்த தொழில் எது?
questions.explanation: 19ஆம் நூற்றாண்டின் கடைசி பாகத்தில், பஞ்சாப், வெள்ளம் போன்ற பகுதிகளில் துணி தொழில் மிக வேகமாக வளர்ந்தது.

questions.question: ‘Status of Women in India through the Ages’ அத்தியாயத்தின் படி, 1935 இல் ‘வீட்டுப்பணியாளர் சட்டம்’ (Maternity Benefit Act) ஏற்றுக்கொள்ளப்பட்டது. இந்த சட்டத்தின் முதன்மை நோக்கம் என்ன?
questions.explanation: Maternity Benefit Act 1935, கர்ப்பிணிப் பெண்களுக்கு வேலை இடத்தில் 12 வார விடுமுறை வழங்குவதைக் குறிக்கிறது.

questions.question: ‘Development of Education in India’ அத்தியாயத்தில், 1882-ல் ‘University of Calcutta’ நிறுவப்பட்ட போது, அதன் முதல் ப்ரொஃபஸர் யார்?
questions.explanation: University of Calcutta-யின் முதல் ப்ரொஃபஸர் ராமசுவாமி அய்யர்.

questions.question: ‘Urban changes during the British period’ அத்தியாயத்தின் படி, 1910-களில் மும்பை நகரில் ‘முன்னணி மருத்துவமனை’ (first modern hospital) எது?
questions.explanation: 1910-ல் மும்பை மகாராஜ் மருத்துவமனை (Sir J. J. Hospital) நிறுவப்பட்டது.

questions.question: ‘Rural Life and Society’ அத்தியாயம் படி, ‘பஞ்சாப்’ பகுதியில் 19ஆம் நூற்றாண்டில் ‘கிராமப்புற கூட்டமைப்பு’ (village community) முக்கியமாக எதைக் கொண்டு நிர்வகிக்கப்பட்டது?
questions.explanation: பஞ்சாப் கிராமங்களில் பஞ்சாயத்து (Panchayat) அமைப்பு சமூக ஒழுங்கை நிர்வகித்தது.

questions.question: ‘Rural Life and Society’ அத்தியாயத்தின் படி, 19‑ஆம் நூற்றாண்டில் தமிழக கிராமங்களில் முக்கியமாகக் காணப்பட்ட இரண்டு சமூக அமைப்புகள் எவை?
questions.explanation: பாடத்தில், ஜாதி மற்றும் குடும்பம் இரண்டும் 19‑ஆம் நூற்றாண்டு கிராம சமூகத்தின் அடிப்படை அமைப்பாக குறிப்பிடப்பட்டுள்ளது.

questions.question: ‘Development of Education in India’ இல், 1857‑க்கு பின் இந்தியாவில் முதன்முதலாக நிறுவப்பட்ட ‘மூன்று‑மூலக் கல்வி’ பள்ளி எது?
questions.explanation: நவாபுரி பள்ளி 1857‑க்கு பின் இந்தியாவில் மூன்று‑மூலக் கல்வி (அறிவியல், கலை, தொழில்) வழங்கும் முதல் பள்ளியாக அறிமுகப்படுத்தப்பட்டது.

questions.question: ‘Development of Industries in India’ படி, 1900‑களில் இந்தியாவில் முதன்முதலாக தொடங்கிய பெரிய தொழில் எது?
questions.explanation: சீமன் தொழில் (ஜூட்) 1901‑இல் பிரிட்டிஷ் நிறுவனம் தொடங்கியது; இது இந்தியாவின் முதல் பெரிய தொழில் ஆகும்.

questions.question: ‘Status of Women in India through the ages’ படி, 1905‑இல் பெண்களுக்கு முதல் முறையாக வழங்கப்பட்ட உரிமை எது?
questions.explanation: 1905‑இல் பெண்களுக்கு முதல் முறையாக உரையாடல் (உச்சரிப்பு) உரிமை வழங்கப்பட்டது; இது பாடத்தில் குறிப்பிடப்பட்டுள்ளது.

questions.question: Which of the following factors most significantly influenced the Portuguese choice of the Coromandel Coast as their first Indian naval base in the early 16th century?
options: The abundance of spices and pepper in the region
Proximity to the monsoon winds for easier navigation
Existing trade networks with local kingdoms
The presence of a large Portuguese population in the area
questions.explanation: The Coromandel Coast was chosen primarily because its location allowed Portuguese ships to take advantage of the monsoon wind patterns, enabling efficient sailing between Goa and the Indian subcontinent.
